citation: Schaffert J, Didehbani N, LoBue C, Hart J Jr, Motes M, Rossetti H, Wilmoth K, Goette W, Lacritz L, Cullum CM. Neurocognitive outcomes of older National Football League retirees. Brain Injury. Published online November 27, 2022. doi:10.1080/02699052.2022.2143567
abstract: This study examined whether head-injury exposure was associated with later-life cognition in retired NFL players age 50 and older. NFL retirees with neuropsychological data were classified as cognitively normal (n=26) or cognitively impaired with mild cognitive impairment or dementia (n=27). Cognitively normal retirees were age- and education-matched to healthy controls, while cognitively impaired retirees were matched to a clinical sample by age, sex, education, and diagnosis. Twenty-nine retirees returned for longitudinal follow-up 13-59 months later. Cognitively normal retirees had slightly lower baseline memory scores than healthy controls, but still performed in the average range. No other group differences were observed, and self-reported concussions, concussions with loss of consciousness, and NFL games played did not predict baseline cognition or cognitive change over time.
tldr: In this older retired NFL sample, concussion count, concussion with loss of consciousness, and games played did not predict later-life neuropsychological performance or change over time. For Baylor football staff, the applied message is to avoid simplistic dose-response assumptions from career exposure alone and to prioritize individualized neurocognitive, mental health, sleep, and medical follow-up.
methods: Prospective longitudinal cohort analysis of retired NFL players age 50 or older from a contact-sport aging study. Retirees completed clinical interview, neuropsychological testing, neuroimaging, neurologic exam, concussion-history interview, and football exposure history. Cognitive status was determined by actuarial MCI criteria and clinical consensus. ANOVAs compared cognitive composites for executive function/attention/speed, language, and memory against matched control groups. Change scores were analyzed in follow-up participants. Linear regressions tested whether number of concussions, concussions with loss of consciousness, and NFL games played predicted baseline scores or change over time.
findings: At baseline, cognitively normal NFL retirees had lower memory scores than healthy controls (mean T-score 50.69 vs 57.08; p=0.005), but the retiree mean remained solidly average and the authors questioned the clinical significance. Cognitively impaired retirees did not differ meaningfully from the clinical comparison group. No significant group differences appeared for executive/attention/speed or language composites. Head-injury exposure variables did not significantly predict baseline cognitive performance or longitudinal change, and position grouping as speed versus non-speed did not alter the main pattern.
limitations: The sample was small, especially for longitudinal follow-up and position subgroup analyses. Concussion history relied on self- or informant report and may be vulnerable to recall bias. Games played is only a rough proxy for subconcussive head impact exposure and does not capture practice exposure, helmet-impact metrics, style of play, or medical management. The clinical comparison sample was retrospective and not recruited alongside the NFL cohort. Healthy controls had above-average memory scores, which may have exaggerated the baseline memory difference.
practicalImplications: For football medicine and performance staff, this paper supports careful individual monitoring rather than assuming that more games or more recalled concussions automatically means measurable late-life decline. Retired and active athletes with cognitive concerns still warrant comprehensive evaluation, but exposure proxies should be interpreted alongside mood, sleep, pain, vascular risk, substance use, family history, and objective neuropsychological data.
athleteDev: Long-term athlete development should include concussion education, honest symptom reporting, medical documentation, and transition planning beyond football. The findings argue for building durable health records and individualized follow-up pathways rather than relying on position or career length as a stand-alone risk profile.
rtp: This is not an acute return-to-play study. Its RTP relevance is indirect: concussion clearance and long-term monitoring should be individualized, documented, and based on clinical recovery rather than assumptions that simple exposure counts can predict future cognitive status.

citation: Grandhisiri T. NFL Draft Modelling: Loss Functional Analysis. arXiv:2504.07291. Published April 11, 2025.
abstract: This arXiv preprint proposes a norm-based framework for evaluating NFL draft pick trade fairness and value distribution. Draft pick values were modeled using an exponential Massey-Thaler style curve, with Weibull-like decay parameters fitted from NFL draft pick trades from 2006-2023. The author compared Mean Absolute Error/L1 and Mean Squared Error/L2 optimization, using L-BFGS-B and bootstrap resampling for 95% confidence intervals. L2/MSE produced a steeper curve that heavily favored early picks and resembled top-heavy trade charts, while L1/MAE produced a flatter curve that assigned more value to later picks. The paper frames these curves as different roster-building philosophies: star-seeking versus depth-oriented.
tldr: The same trade data can imply very different draft values depending on the loss function. MSE makes premium picks look much more valuable; MAE spreads value deeper into the draft. For Baylor staff, the useful lens is decision discipline: draft capital models should match the team's development philosophy and should not be treated as objective truth without considering player outcomes and roster needs.
methods: The paper used a public draft trade dataset filtered to 2006-2023. Pick numbers were parsed into upward and downward trade components, while future picks, players, and cash were excluded to simplify immediate pick-for-pick valuation. A two-parameter exponential value curve was fitted under L1/MAE and L2/MSE loss functions with L-BFGS-B optimization. Bootstrap resampling generated empirical parameter distributions and 95% confidence intervals. Fitted curves were compared with each other and with the Jimmy Johnson draft value chart.
findings: MSE/L2 optimization sharply discounted later picks and emphasized early picks, reflecting a top-heavy strategy. MAE/L1 optimization declined more gradually and assigned substantially more value to mid- and late-round selections, aligning with depth and long-term development logic. Parameter estimates differed by loss function, and confidence intervals showed greater uncertainty for later picks. The L2 curve more closely resembled the traditional Jimmy Johnson chart, while the L1 curve likely overstated late-round pick value relative to observed player impact probabilities.
limitations: The analysis excludes future picks, players, cash, and other trade assets that are common in real NFL transactions. It models pick value rather than actual player performance, salary-cap value, roster fit, injury risk, or development outcomes. The data and framework are useful for trade structure but do not establish which curve produces better football decisions. The paper is an arXiv preprint and not presented as peer-reviewed in the local PDF.
practicalImplications: For football operations and sport science, draft value should be connected to a development model. If a program believes it can identify, develop, and retain later-round traits, a flatter valuation logic may fit. If a decision-maker prioritizes rare elite traits, a steeper curve may fit. Either way, staff should audit whether trade models are aligned with scouting confidence, positional scarcity, injury history, and the organization's ability to develop players.
athleteDev: The paper reinforces that player development changes the practical value of draft capital. A team with strong developmental infrastructure may rationally value more selections and depth, while a team seeking immediate high-end traits may accept top-heavy costs. For Baylor, this supports tracking how physical traits, coachability, durability, and role fit convert into multi-year value.
rtp: This is not an injury or RTP study. Its indirect RTP relevance is roster planning: pick valuation models should account for medical risk and availability when teams compare one premium prospect with multiple developmental options.

citation: Yurko R, Nguyen Q, Pelechrinis K. NFL Ghosts: A framework for evaluating defender positioning with conditional density estimation. arXiv:2406.17220. Published June 25, 2024.
abstract: This preprint introduces a public framework for evaluating NFL defender positioning and trajectory against a distribution of hypothetical baseline 'ghost' defenders. Using NFL Big Data Bowl 2021 tracking data from the 2018 season, the authors focused on nearest defender positioning at the moment of catch on completed passes outside the end zone. Random forests for conditional density estimation modeled both the receiver yards-after-catch distribution and the two-dimensional location distribution of ghost defenders. The framework converts observed versus ghost positioning into expected play value changes. In an example play, Jaire Alexander's observed location was worth about -0.93 expected points relative to the ghost baseline. Across 10,363 plays, player and team metrics showed meaningful relationships with YAC allowed and pass-defense EPA.
tldr: This paper turns tracking data into an interpretable defender-positioning score by asking: how much better or worse was the real defender than a plausible ghost defender? For Baylor football staff, the approach points toward evaluating coverage and pursuit with context, not just outcomes like completion, YAC, or tackles.
methods: The authors used 2018 NFL Big Data Bowl tracking data recorded at 10 Hz. After filtering to completed passes outside the end zone with complete features, 10,363 plays were analyzed. Features included adjusted x/y locations, speed, movement direction, orientation, distance to receiver, first-down distance, and relative offensive and defensive positions at catch or pass release. Random forests for conditional density estimation with 500 trees estimated receiver YAC distributions and ghost defender location distributions. Expected play value at catch was calculated by integrating modeled YAC outcomes through an expected-points utility function, then comparing observed defender value with the ghost baseline.
findings: The example play showed that the observed nearest defender was positioned in a highly favorable defensive location, producing a change of about -0.93 expected points compared with the ghost baseline. Across the season, the top accumulated defender values included mostly cornerbacks, with Adoree' Jackson leading at -7.23 total expected points across 56 plays. Average change in expected play value correlated moderately with average YAC allowed (overall r=0.65), and team-level ghost metrics correlated with pass-defense EPA on total and average scales. The framework separated process quality from raw outcomes, such as identifying defenders who were positioned well but still allowed YAC.
limitations: The model evaluates only the nearest defender at the moment of catch, which may not always be the true coverage responsibility. It simplifies football by moving one defender while holding teammates fixed, so team scheme and coordinated coverage are not fully represented. The analysis considers completed passes and YAC after the catch, not target prevention, incompletions, interceptions, route leverage before the catch, or full continuous-time coverage. Position and coverage type were not fully conditioned in the main ghost model.
practicalImplications: For Baylor staff, this framework suggests a way to grade defensive backs and linebackers on spatial process quality rather than only results. Practice and game review could use similar logic to identify whether defenders are arriving at high-value locations, whether poor outcomes are tackling failures versus positioning failures, and whether coverage calls consistently place athletes in favorable leverage.
athleteDev: Athlete development can use ghost-style evaluation to teach leverage, angle, speed control, and pursuit positioning. Instead of telling a defender only that he allowed YAC, coaches can show whether his body position, distance, and trajectory at the catch were better or worse than a context-aware baseline. This supports individualized technical feedback for coverage, tackling setup, and spatial awareness.
rtp: This is not an RTP study, but the framework could support return-to-performance decisions for defensive players. A returning DB or LB could be compared with his pre-injury positioning, speed, and trajectory benchmarks in coverage drills before full game exposure.

citation: Evans BA, Adams C, Pitts JD, Lingerfelt D. NFL Quarterback Development—The Role of Competing Quarterbacks, Rookie Playing Time, and Team Quality. The American Economist. 2024;0(0):1-16. doi:10.1177/05694345241292611
abstract: This sports economics study examined how competing quarterback quality, rookie playing time, and drafting team quality relate to NFL quarterback development. Using Pro-Football-Reference data for quarterbacks drafted from 1994-2017, the authors analyzed 277 quarterbacks after excluding expansion-team cases and players drafted as quarterbacks but expected to play another position. Outcomes included games started, passing yards, and approximate value during the rookie year, seasons two through five, and after season five. The analysis found that quarterbacks playing alongside a Pro Bowl quarterback early in their career had worse mid- and late-career outcomes, quarterbacks drafted by worse teams tended to have better later-career trajectories, and rookie playing time showed evidence of reducing later-career outcomes.
tldr: QB development appears strongly shaped by context outside the player's control. Early rookie starts were not clearly beneficial long term, Pro Bowl incumbent competition was associated with worse future outcomes, and being drafted by a bad team predicted better later opportunity and production. For Baylor staff, developmental timing and environment matter as much as raw talent.
methods: Cross-sectional econometric analysis of quarterbacks drafted from 1994-2017 using Pro-Football-Reference data. The sample included 277 drafted quarterbacks after exclusions. Dependent variables included rookie games started, games started in seasons two through five, games started after season five, passing yards over the same windows, and approximate value. Independent variables included draft pick, age, incumbent quarterback Pro Bowl measures before/during/after the rookie season, incumbent age, drafting-team wins before and during the rookie year, and prior playing-time variables. Models included team and draft-year fixed effects.
findings: Earlier draft picks were more likely to start as rookies, and teams with worse prior records created more rookie opportunity. After season five, each additional prior-year team win was associated with fewer later starts, passing yards, and approximate value. Playing alongside a Pro Bowl quarterback as a rookie was associated with about 9.2 fewer starts after season five and lower later passing output. More games started in seasons two through five predicted later starts, but rookie starts had a negative coefficient for later starts and approximate value, giving modest support to the idea that too much rookie playing time can hurt long-term development.
limitations: The study is observational and cannot prove causality. Quarterback outcomes are influenced by coaching quality, injuries, offensive line, scheme, roster turnover, salary-cap decisions, and unobserved talent evaluation. Approximate value and passing yards are useful but imperfect measures of quarterback quality. The sample ends with the 2017 draft to allow at least five seasons, so newer developmental models and rule environments may not be fully captured.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the paper supports deliberate quarterback development plans that manage timing, confidence, game exposure, and support quality. Simply playing a young quarterback early is not automatically development. Staff should separate practice readiness, game-plan protection, offensive line quality, opponent load, and psychological readiness when deciding how quickly to expand a QB's live role.
athleteDev: Quarterback development should be staged. Young QBs need reps, but their early exposure should be structured so they build decision speed and leadership without being overloaded by poor protection, low-support rosters, or unstable role definition. Competition can be useful, but sitting behind a high-status incumbent may reduce meaningful reps and delay relationship-building with starters.
rtp: This is not an injury RTP study. Its RTP relevance is developmental: after injury or time away, quarterback reintegration should consider depth-chart context, practice reps with starters, offensive support, and staged exposure rather than relying only on medical clearance.

citation: Quinn M, Painter DF, Ahn BJ, Byrne RA, Testa EJ, Albright A, Tabaddor RR, DeFroda SF. NFL Quarterbacks with Ulnar Collateral Ligament Injuries Have High Return to Play Rates, but Older Players Have Inferior Post-Injury Performance. Arthroscopy, Sports Medicine, and Rehabilitation. 2024. doi:10.1016/j.asmr.2024.100954
abstract: This Level IV therapeutic case series characterized NFL quarterback ulnar collateral ligament injuries from 1991-2023. Using the Pro Sports Transactions Archive, team and league reports, Associated Press-affiliated media reports, and Pro Football Reference, the authors identified 21 UCL injuries in 20 quarterbacks. They recorded injury mechanism, age, treatment strategy, return-to-play time, and performance from the season before injury through two seasons after injury. Sixteen players returned to play, with mean RTP around 163-166 days overall. UCL repair or reconstruction required substantially longer RTP than nonoperative management (about 359 days vs 98 days; p=0.014). Injuries after 2006 were associated with better post-injury performance, while older age at injury was associated with worse post-injury performance.
tldr: NFL QBs usually returned after UCL injury, but surgery took much longer and older quarterbacks had worse post-injury performance. For Baylor football, elbow UCL RTP should separate medical return from performance restoration, especially for older or high-mileage throwers.
methods: Retrospective case series of NFL quarterbacks with UCL injuries from January 1, 1991 to February 1, 2023. Injuries were identified from the Pro Sports Transactions Archive and verified with team reports, NFL.com, Associated Press-affiliated reports, prior literature, and Pro Football Reference. Variables included contact versus non-contact mechanism, operative versus nonoperative management, days to RTP, age, body size, and performance statistics. Principal component analysis compressed games played, quarterback rating, completion percentage, touchdowns, and interceptions into an overall performance metric. Statistical comparisons assessed management strategy, injury era, age, and mechanism.
findings: The authors identified 21 injuries in 20 quarterbacks, with 10 contact and 11 non-contact injuries. Thirteen injuries were managed nonoperatively and eight underwent UCL repair or reconstruction. Sixteen players returned for at least one more NFL season; nine played at least two more post-injury seasons. Mean RTP was about 163 days overall, but operative cases averaged 359 days compared with 98.25 days for nonoperative cases (p=0.014). Performance did not differ significantly by management strategy, but injuries from 2006 onward showed better post-injury performance (p=0.041). Increasing age at injury correlated with worse post-injury performance (p=0.048).
limitations: The study is retrospective and depends on public injury reporting, which limits injury grading, rehab details, PRP or biologic use, surgical technique, and exact medical decision-making. The sample is small because NFL quarterback UCL injuries are rare, reducing power for subgroup analyses. Findings may not generalize to college quarterbacks, non-quarterbacks, or other throwing sports. Post-injury performance was not able to fully account for team context, coaching changes, offensive line quality, role changes, or personal factors.
practicalImplications: For Baylor sports medicine, quarterback elbow UCL cases should be managed with a clear distinction between RTP and return to prior performance. Nonoperative cases may return faster when injury severity permits, while repair/reconstruction timelines are closer to a full-season planning problem. Older throwers may need more conservative workload progression, objective throwing-quality monitoring, and realistic performance expectations.
athleteDev: Quarterbacks need long-term elbow capacity development, not just symptom management. Throwing volume, recovery, mechanics, shoulder and trunk contribution, forearm strength, and workload spikes should be monitored across the year. Younger quarterbacks may have better performance resilience after injury, but all throwers benefit from proactive arm-care systems and staged exposure.
rtp: RTP after QB UCL injury should be staged by tissue status, symptoms, throwing mechanics, velocity/accuracy tolerance, workload accumulation, and competitive performance. Nonoperative management in this cohort averaged about 98 days to RTP, while UCL repair/reconstruction averaged about 359 days. Clearance should include progressive throwing, practice integration, contact-risk planning, and performance monitoring after return.
